*   •Artificial intelligence-machine learning (AI-ML) enhances green hydrogen production, storage, and distribution cycles. Green hydrogen is a cleaner source to replace fossil-based fuels and is critical in the global shift toward energy production to combat climate change. These multidomain applications of AI are quintessentially illustrative of AI's revolutionary capabilities in redefining hydrogen-based energy systems to support sustainable energy technologies . Review article

AI-ML techniques for green hydrogen: A comprehensive review
===========================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Mamta Motiramani a, Priyanshi Solanki a, Vidhi Patel a, Tamanna Talreja a, Nainsiben Patel a, Divya Chauhan b, Alok Kumar Singh c

Show more

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Artificial intelligence-machine learning (AI-ML) enhances green hydrogen production, storage, and distribution cycles. 
*   •AI-driven detection systems help in avoiding hydrogen leaks for safe operation. 
*   •ML predicts energy demand and optimizes hydrogen storage. 
*   •AI-ML enhances the viability of hydrogen by reducing costs and increasing production. 

Abstract
--------

Green hydrogen is a cleaner source to replace fossil-based fuels and is critical in the global shift toward energy production to combat climate change. This review of embedding artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) in the value chain of green hydrogen outlines the significant potential for full transformation. These include optimizing the utilization of renewable sources of energy, improving electrolysis process, hydrogen storage in the salt cavern that has better condition, and smarter systems in distribution side with inexpensive logistics. In this, it nullifies leak risks and safeguards the safety operations with detection using AI. Consequently, it positions the paper emphasizing AI-ML approaches demonstrating significant advancements in efficiency and sustainability in green hydrogen technology.

1. Introduction
---------------

Global warming has caused an alarming temperature to rise over the last 2 decades, accounting for almost 490,000 heat-related deaths every year and exacerbating the water shortage vulnerability among more than 933 million urban dwellers. This climatic instability, now fueled by the greenhouse effects resulting from climate change and global warming, has caused extreme climatic events like increased heatwaves, droughts, and floods, which have caused significant disasters in human societies, ecosystems, and economics. The alteration of regional hydrological cycles and the escalation of flood risks have become a critical concern as about 1.81 billion people are now at heightened risk of extreme flooding [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib2). The world should yield to the evolution of a cleaner and greener energy future. According to the statement by Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, the environmental crisis remains a serious issue concerning our society [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib3). Since the 20th century, human-caused greenhouse gases (GHGs) have been responsible for universal climate change [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib4). To reduce the GHGs emissions, it was essential to shift toward renewable energy sources instead of fossils [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib1), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib7). The European Union aims at reducing emissions by as much as 40% in 2030 compared to the 1990 level. It will also try to reach renewable source energy share close to 32% [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib8), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib11). This is possible based on guidelines offered by the Paris Agreement, curtail global CO2 emissions by an approximation of over 90%. Renewable sources are anticipated to contribute essentially at 41%, with hydrogen playing a significant part in decarbonizing different sectors, which include transportation, manufacturing, and households [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib12). By 2050, hydrogen has the potential to supplant strong fills and normal gas, conceivably expending up to 530 million tons [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib11), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib14).

Hydrogen is segmented into several types depending upon the byproducts produced during its production and the sources of energy used, and they include green, gray, blue, gold, brown, white, turquoise, pink, yellow, etc. [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib16), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib19). Gray hydrogen has been produced from natural gas without capturing the emissions, thereby considered responsible as an important portion of CO2 emissions. Whereas blue hydrogen is better than gray hydrogen in that it is purportedly produced with the use of storage technology and carbon capture to minimize emissions, production processes are still the same [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib20), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib21). The production of green hydrogen occurs by harnessing renewable energy resources, producing electricity, water, and heat as its only outputs [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib11), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib22), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib23). That opens up a closed-loop system, making minimal emissions of GHG and placing green hydrogen as a clean, stable, and flexible source of energy [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib2), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib24), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib25), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib27).

The global green hydrogen market was worth about USD 6.49 billion in 2024. Projections for this market indicate a compound annual growth rate of more than 31% through 2032. By 2030, global production will be about 63,750 tons per day, which is equivalent to 150 GW capacity [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib3). The production capacity for green hydrogen in the next 5 years is expected to multiply by 12-fold. Green hydrogen development by those countries would not only reduce reliance on imported fuels but also accelerate their serious efforts toward the achievement of environmental sustainability goals [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib28).

There is an imminent need to find affordable, reliable, environmentally sustainable, and carbon-free sources of energy [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib29). Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) are front-runners in trying to meet these needs, especially by optimizing the value chain for green hydrogen [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib30). Both technologies depend on past data to create forecasting models to amplify its scalability, thereby strengthening the reliability and resilience of energy systems [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib31). AI models, such as deep learning architectures, can infer complex interdependencies in large datasets and simulate human cognitive processes to enhance the effectiveness of systems. In parallel, ML algorithms autonomously continue improving their predictive accuracy in a self-learning algorithm by importing new data points into models, which helps model intricate physical and operational properties of energy systems [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib32), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib33).

AI algorithms have been instrumental in enriching the accuracy and efficiency in the performance of different energy production systems. By integrating historical data with meteorological predictions, AI systems can develop very accurate predictive models for the output of energy [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib15). One of the most striking application aspects of AI is related to electrolysis for hydrogen production, that is, AI-based optimization of the performance of electrolyzers. AI is utilized in the advancement of new catalysts for hydrogen production, enabling inventions in energy production techniques [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib34). Within the field of hydrogen fuel cells, AI enables real-time operational optimization through predicting the execution of microbial and solid oxide fuel cells, simultaneously factoring in other important parameters such as CO2 hydrogenation and hydrogen power density [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib6), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib35). Computational methods in advanced fuzzy logic, for instance, are used to fine-tune the ignition times for hydrogen-fueled vehicles, which highlights AI's potential to improve performance efficiency. AI becomes an essential component in optimizing hydrogen storage and purification systems, improving production performance with declining costs concurrently. These multidomain applications of AI are quintessentially illustrative of AI's revolutionary capabilities in redefining hydrogen-based energy systems to support sustainable energy technologies [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib36).

ML is the core area of AI; it has a focus on feature learning by which systems improve themselves without human intervention [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib22), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib32). The ML algorithms have highly efficient techniques to enhance hydrogen storage while decreasing hazards associated with hydrogen leakage [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib37). ML models have been used widely to study specific material characteristics in databases, which provide better material performance forecasts, identify material patterns, and speed up the introduction of new material. The approach simultaneously saves both cost and time while providing reliable guidance for researchers [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib38). Hydrogen production can be predicted in time and decreases the cost using ML by utilizing the wind data combined with historical data through a proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzer [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib39). ML can be applied in electric vehicles to increase the feedback gain of hydrogen gas reformers via the supply of hydrogen for the PEM fuel cells [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib40).

While green hydrogen has tremendous potential, many obstacles stand in the way of further adoption and integration into the global energy picture. Green hydrogen constitutes almost 0.1% of worldwide vitality utilization at present because it continues to be within the exploratory stage [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib41). Scaling up production and storage is limited by significant barriers toward the economical delivery of hydrogen. Whereas AI-ML technologies have shown immense promise for optimizing hydrogen systems, their complete integration into the hydrogen value chain is constrained. Besides, robust solutions for leak detection in hydrogen systems, safety assessment, and predictive risk assessment need more study. These avenues need to be critically actionable for hydrogen energy systems to develop as a sustainable energy option and realize the sustainability goals globally [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib37), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib42), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib43).

This paper discusses the green hydrogen cycle such as production of hydrogen from renewable sources, storage in underground salt caverns where it can be possible to have a high flow, issues regarding leakage and safety issues at each stage, transportation by maritime means, roadways, or pipelines. On the other hand, AI-ML based methods help in allowing the hydrogen cycle to fulfill the objective of a low-carbon economy. [Scheme 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#fig0095) outlines key issues and opportunities arising in the fast-paced sector of hydrogen energy.

![Image 12: Scheme 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-sc1.jpg)

Scheme 1. Development in hydrogen cycle, production, storage, leakage and safety, and distribution through AI-ML. AI = artificial intelligence; ML = machine learning.

2. Production
-------------

Hydrogen production methods are divided into physical, electrochemical, and chemical processes, the core of which is technologies around electrolysis [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib13), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib44), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib46). [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#fig0005) presents numerous techniques of green hydrogen production like photocatalysis, photoelectrochemical water splitting, photocatalytic hydrogen production, bioenergy, biological photolysis, microalgal biohydrogen production, and electrolysis.

![Image 13: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (121KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (121KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Various methods to produce green hydrogen.

As illustrated in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#fig0010), the mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen production is a subject where electron excitation from valence band to conductive band under light irradiation produces electron-hole pairs. The charges are responsible for redox reactions as electrons reduce water to hydrogen and holes oxidize water to oxygen. The separation of these charges is essential for high photocatalytic performance. Structural engineering of niobium complexes, such as doping with nitrogen or noble metals like Pt and Ag, decreases the bandgap so that visible light can be absorbed. The strategies, including the formation of heterojunctions and Z-schemes, enhance the charge transfer efficiency and reduce electron-hole recombination, enhancing H₂ production. Several niobium-based catalysts have been developed. Perovskite niobates (e.g., KNbO₃) are of particular interest because of their strong ability to harvest light and preferably accommodated dipole moments. Layered niobates have abundant surface areas and active sites, which arise from their nanosheet morphologies and favor effective charge separation. Nb₂O₅ structures in the form of nanowires and nanobelts exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity, due to superior light absorption and enhanced mobility of electrons. Dye-doped niobates further extend the light absorption spectrum into the visible wavelengths, using organic dyes to enhance photocatalytic activity [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib47). Another study [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib48) presented a new metal-free photocatalyst, Ni-Si/MgO—an efficient visible light active water-splitting photocatalyst to produce hydrogen (H₂). Addressing the current shortcomings associated with the catalyst systems from noble metals that are expensive and not abundant. A 1-pot solid-state reduction process was used to fabricate ultrathin, hierarchically porous nanosheets showing improved efficiency and application in photocatalytic processes. Utilizing this catalyst system, a hydrogen evolution rate of 714 µmol h⁻¹ has been achieved, which is much better compared to several other semiconductor systems without the use of drilling agents, pH's, and voltage evasions.

![Image 14: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (150KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (150KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949821X25000158-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Mechanism of solar-driven water splitting on a semiconductor.

Copyrights are taken from Islam et al. [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#bib47).

The Ni-Si/MgO catalyst was comprised of nanosheets with an ultrathin structure which helps in effective light harvesting, short diffusion paths for electrons, and prompt charge separation. This setup allows the electrons and holes generated by the light to be kept apart from each other in space, lessening recombination and fostering hydrogen evolution. Active functionalized surface groups that include Si-H and Si-OH promote further water dissociation and proton reduction, with nickel acting as the site for reduction. The bandgap is adjusted to fall within the range 2.48 of 2.58 eV to enhance the absorption of visible light range. Even after several reaction cycles, the structural and chemical integrity of the catalyst has been demonstrated using X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and electron microscopy, among others. In [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949821X25000158#fig0015), the overall arrangement of equipment and instruments for conducting experiments on the photochemical splitting of water is illustrated. In the system, aside from the 450 W xenon lamp that radiates visible emission greater than 400 nm in wavelength, a Pyrex reaction vessel equipped with pure water and a Ni-Si/MgO photocatalyst is placed. A thermostatic water jacket ensures that the temperature remains constant during the reaction. Also employed is a magnetic stirrer to aid in the mixing of the treated liquid. The hydrogen evolved in the reactor is carried by argon to gas chromatography with a thermal conductivity detector for further in-line measurement of the hydrogen content. The device demonstrates the use of the Ni-Si/MgO system in a practical situation without any additional sacrificial agents or driving voltage is required. Results obtained from this study indicate that the photocatalytic activity of Ni-Si/MgO can be improved when used in conjunction with other semiconducting materials, which could represent a viable direction of research toward more efficient solar synthesis of hydrogen evolution capacity.

# How AI guided the development of green hydrogen production: in the case of solid oxide electrolysis cell?

![](data:image/png;base64,)
![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Baoyin Yuan1,#

### Xiaohan Zhang2,#

### Siyu Ye2,\*

## Abstract

The development of efficient and stable hydrogen production technologies is crucial for global clean energy transition. Solid oxide electrolysis cells (SOECs) have emerged as a promising technology for green hydrogen production due to their high efficiency, low-cost catalysts, and excellent adaptability to renewable energy sources. However, significant challenges remain in materials design, interface engineering, and system integration. This perspective reviews recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI)-guided SOEC development, focusing on machine learning approaches for design of key materials. Furthermore, we highlight how AI technologies can address the key challenges in both single-cell performances and system-level integration with renewable energy sources. Looking forward, we outline strategic directions for advancing AI-driven SOEC development toward commercial implementation, which may offer valuable insights and experiences within the field of energy conversion and storage.

## Graphical Abstract

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ea39f39d-c55d-47d9-a71e-8638a3799c9b/jmi40106-coverimg.big.jpg)

## Keywords

## MAIN TEXT

In the context of global climate change, clean energy transition has become a core development task for countries worldwide. The use of fossil fuels has led to massive greenhouse gas emissions, exacerbating global warming and environmental degradation[[1](#B1)-[3](#B3)]. Hydrogen energy, with its high energy density and zero-emission characteristics, particularly green hydrogen produced through water electrolysis using renewable energy sources (such as wind and solar power), has garnered significant attention[[4](#B4),[5](#B5)]. However, the supply of renewable energy exhibits significant intermittency and instability. For instance, solar power generation heavily depends on sunlight conditions, with power generation capacity dramatically decreasing or even completely stopping during cloudy days, rainy weather, or at night when there is insufficient light. This intermittency poses severe challenges to the stable operation of water electrolysis hydrogen production equipment, necessitating the development of highly adaptable hydrogen production technologies[[6](#B6),[7](#B7)].

The development of hydrogen production techniques for water electrolysis has gone through an evolutionary process from alkaline water electrolyzers (AWE), proton exchange membrane electrolyzers (PEMEC) to solid oxide electrolysis cell (SOEC)[[7](#B7),[8](#B8)]. AWE, as the earliest technology to be industrialized, has the advantages of mature technology and relatively low cost. However, AWE has several key limitations: firstly, it requires the use of precious metal catalysts, such as Pt, to reduce the overpotential of the electrode reaction, which significantly increases the system cost; secondly, the low operating current density (typically between 0.2-0.4 A·cm-2) limits the hydrogen production efficiency; most importantly, it has a slow startup and shut-down process with poor dynamic response, making it difficult to adapt to the fluctuating characteristics of renewable energy sources[[9](#B9),[10](#B10)]. PEMEC has achieved a significant improvement in performance by utilizing proton exchange membranes as the electrolyte. The technology operates below 100 °C with high proton conductivity and chemical stability, rapid startup, excellent dynamic response performance, and current densities up to 1-2 A·cm-2. However, the catalysts of PEMEC are expensive (use of Pt and Ir) and extremely sensitive to impurities, requiring stringent gas purification[[11](#B11),[12](#B12)].

SOEC operating at 400-1,000 °C has high energy conversion efficiency (more than 80%) due to the faster electrochemical reaction kinetics and lower overpotential under high temperatures. Besides, SOEC can operate at current densities of 2-5 A·cm-2 or even higher with low-cost oxides as catalysts, which are abundant in resources[[4](#B4),[13](#B13)-[15](#B15)]. Importantly, it can maintain stable operation under a wide range of power fluctuation conditions, showing excellent adaptability. Lastly, it can be system-integrated with high-temperature processes, such as industrial waste heat recovery, carbon capture, and so on, to further improve energy utilization efficiency. These advantages make SOEC a key technology for solving the problem of efficient utilization of renewable energy.

Despite the significant advantages of SOEC technology, it still faces many challenges on the way to commercialization. Firstly, the design of key materials, including anodes, cathodes and electrolytes, is the main bottleneck restricting the performances of SOEC, whose stability, catalytic activity, and ionic conductivity at high temperatures and high current densities still need to be further improved. As for anode materials, Sm0.5Sr0.5CoO3-δ (SSC)[[16](#B16),[17](#B17)], (LaSr)CoO3-δ (LSC)[[18](#B18)], La0.6Sr0.4Co0.2Fe0.8O3-δ (LSCF)[[19](#B19)], and Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ (BSCF)[[20](#B20)] are the widely used anodes for SOECs. As for the electrolyte, perovskite oxides BaZr1-*x*-*y*Ce*x*M*y*O3 (BZCM, M = Y, Yb, *etc.*) and yttria-stabilised zirconia (YSZ) are the most widely used electrolytes for SOEC[[21](#B21)-[24](#B24)]. Besides, oxides with A*n*+1B*n*O3*n*+1 Ruddlesden-Popper structure, A2B2O5 Brownmillerite structure, ReMO4 structure, A2B2O7 structure and A10-*x*B6O26±δ apatite-type structure are also sometimes reported[[25](#B25)]. Ni-based cermets are used as typical anode materials, such as Ni-YSZ and Ni-BZCM cermets[[26](#B26)]. In addition, much effort still needs to be injected into the interfacial design of SOEC. Furthermore, the operating efficiency of SOEC is affected by various factors including temperature, pressure, current density, *etc.* The problem of how to optimize these parameters under fluctuating renewable energy conditions is also an urgent issue[[27](#B27)]. If the solution to the above issues relies on traditional material development processes, there are often problems such as long material development cycles and high costs. The rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) technology has provided new ideas and approaches to address these challenges. AI has demonstrated powerful capabilities in data processing, model building and optimization, and is able to effectively analyze and mine the complex relationships between material properties and structure. Through algorithms such as machine learning (ML) and deep learning, researchers can quickly screen potential high-performance materials and optimize their design[[28](#B28)-[34](#B34)].

Currently, some advances have been achieved, such as design of key materials and prediction of stability, relating to ML in SOEC[[35](#B35)-[37](#B37)]. As for the electrolyte, Hyodo *et al.* developed a gradient-augmented regression ML model to study the hydration structure-property relationship for perovskite oxides[[38](#B38)]. Gradient boosted regressor (GBR) model was trained by considering 80 descriptors based on the chemical compositions of perovskite oxides. After training, hydration structure-property maps were constructed, serving as a powerful tool to screen candidate materials from a vast number of compounds, thereby significantly accelerating the discovery process of proton-conducting oxides. Surprisingly, a new and outstanding material, SrSn0.8Sc0.2O3-δ, processing the proton conductivity of 1.4 × 10-3 S·cm-1 at 380 °C, was identified from 8,613 hypothetical perovskite oxides [[Figure 1A](#fig1)]. This research provides a new avenue for the discovery of novel proton-conducting electrolytes for SOEC. Recently, Luo *et al*. reported that BaSnxCe0.8-xYb0.2O3-δ (BSCYb) exhibits higher proton conductivity (0.02 S·cm-1) and chemical stability (1,000 h), which is screened from 932 oxide candidate materials by calculating parameters such as oxygen vacancy formation energy, hydration energy [[Figure 1B](#fig1)], and adsorption energies of H2O and CO2 [[Figure 1C](#fig1)][[39](#B39)]. The current density of SOEC with BSCYb is 2.62 A·cm-2 at 600 °C and 1.3 V, ranking top among the reported cells[[40](#B40)].

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ea39f39d-c55d-47d9-a71e-8638a3799c9b/jmi40106.fig.1.jpg)

Figure 1. (A) Schematic workflow for the construction and discovery of proton conducting oxides using data-driven structure-property diagrams. This figure is quoted with permission from Hyodo *et al.*[[38](#B38)]; (B) High-throughput calculation results of hydration energy for BaCeO3-derived, BaZrO3-derived, BaHfO3-derived, and BaSnO3-derived oxides; (C) H2O and CO2 adsorption energies at 0 K. This figure is quoted with permission from Luo *et al.*[[39](#B39)].

Compared to the electrolytes, anodes of SOEC have received more attention[[11](#B11),[35](#B35)-[37](#B37)]. In 2022, Wang *et al.* developed accurate and interpretable ML models with random forest and boosting (XGBoost) algorithms, *etc.*, to predict the hydrated proton concentration of oxides for the fast-screening promising anodes [[Figure 2A](#fig2)-[C](#fig2)], which is the first time to screen anode with the assistance of AI[[36](#B36),[41](#B41)]. Initially, a database consisting of 792 samples was established, where 29 features, including temperature, water pressure, *etc.*, were designated as input variables, and the hydrated proton concentrations served as the target variable. After the model was trained, (La,Ca)(Co,Ni)O3 family oxides were screened as the promising anodes of SOECs from thousands of perovskite oxides [[Figure 2D](#fig2)]. For instance, the current density of the cell with a LaCo0.9Ni0.1O3 anode achieved 1.44 A·cm-2 at 1.3 V and 650 °C[[41](#B41)]. After that, Zhai *et al.* introduced the ionic Lewis acid strength (ISA) - defined as the ability of ionic Lewis acids to accept electron pairs - as an effective physical descriptor of artificial neural networks (ANNs) model to design high oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity of perovskite oxides[[11](#B11)]. The screened oxide Sr0.9Cs0.1Co0.9Nb0.1O3 (SCCN) shows small area-specific resistance (ASR) - the resistance encountered when current passes through a unit area of electrode material - (0.0101 Ω·cm-2) and good stability (800 h) [[Figure 3A](#fig3) and [B](#fig3)]. In 2024, Li *et al*. predicted the oxygen vacancy concentration of Co-based and Fe-based perovskite oxides in a wide temperature range for the design of oxygen electrodes [[Figure 3C](#fig3)][[42](#B42)]. They revealed that the predicted oxygen vacancy concentration is consistent well with ORR activity [[Figure 3D](#fig3) and [E](#fig3)]. This work provides a ML-guided route for developing oxygen electrocatalysts suitable for operation at different temperatures[[42](#B42)].

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ea39f39d-c55d-47d9-a71e-8638a3799c9b/jmi40106.fig.2.jpg)

Figure 2. (A) Schematic diagram of the research process of screening anode of SOEC using ML models; (B) Comparison of actual and predicted values by the model, and the residuals of each predicted value by comparing with the actual values in XGBoost model; (C) General schematic diagram of the gradient boosting decision tree algorithm; (D) Part of the predicted hydrated proton concentration results. SOEC: Solid oxide electrolysis cell; ML: machine learning.

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ea39f39d-c55d-47d9-a71e-8638a3799c9b/jmi40106.fig.3.jpg)

Figure 3. (A) Feature importance of the ionic descriptors based on the sensitivity analysis of the ANN model; (B) ASR values of Sr0.9Cs0.1Co0.9Nb0.1O3 in a symmetrical cell measured for 800 h in air at 550 °C; (C) ML-assisted process for predicting oxygen vacancy concentrations; The ORR activity at 450 °C as a function of the (D) measured and (E) ML-predicted oxygen vacancy concentration. A-site and B-site: In cathode materials, ABO3-type perovskite oxides, such as LaCoO3, LaCrO3, LaNiO3, *etc.*, are commonly studied. For the doping of these oxides, the cell where the La element is located is generally referred to as the A-site, and the cell where Co/Cr/Ni is located is the B-site. ISA: Ionic Lewis acids are usually substances capable of accepting electron pairs. ASR: This refers to the resistance encountered when the current passes through a unit area of electrode material. Tolerance factor (t): A parameter used to measure the relationship between the size of different ionic radii and the stability of the crystal structure in a given crystal structure. Ionic electronegativity: A measure of an atom’s ability to attract electrons when forming an ionic bond. It reflects the combined effect of an atom’s electron affinity and ionization potential in a molecule or crystal. ANN: Artificial neural network; ASR: area-specific resistance; ML: machine learning; ORR: oxygen reduction reaction.

Despite the significant progress in AI-guided SOEC research and development, AI technology still needs to play a more active role in solving practical problems in terms of improving single-cell performance and suitability for renewable energy sources. The bottlenecks we are currently facing and the corresponding solutions can be approached from the following two levels: At the single-cell level, firstly, key material performances, including insufficient catalytic activity, long-term stability to be improved, low electrolyte ionic conductivity, component compatibility issues, *etc.*, should be improved. Secondly, interface engineering challenges are manifested in poor component suitability and complex interfacial reaction mechanisms. Thirdly, material degradation mechanisms have not been fully elucidated, especially at high current density, affecting long-term system reliability.

To address these challenges, AI-driven solutions breakthrough in three main directions: (1) Materials design optimization through deep data analysis and ML algorithms, mining existing material databases, and identifying key factors for performances, including ionic/electronic conductivity, thermal expansion co-efficiency, catalytic activity, porosity, *etc.*, predicting these performances of new material combinations, and guiding the optimization of material compositions and structures; (2) ML can be used to modify the interfaces in the cell. For instance, enhancing the compatibility and chemical reactivity between the electrolyte and anode, identifying optimal compositions, thicknesses, and preparation conditions for functional layers in between the electrolyte and anode, which plays a crucial role in reducing interfacial resistance, improving charge transfer, and enhancing overall cell performance; (3) Degradation mechanism analysis: analyze microstructure evolution, simulate electrochemical reaction process, predict material behavior under different working conditions, establish theoretical guidance framework, and promote iterative innovation of key materials.

At the system level, adaptation between SOEC and renewable energy faces three core issues: power fluctuation adaptability (unstable output power, slow response speed, frequent start-stop affecting the life), thermal management (lack of precision in temperature control, thermal cycling stress, low efficiency), and system control (complex synergistic, difficult to optimize the parameters, lagging failure warning). AI addresses these challenges by building systematic solutions: (1) constructing the prediction models to analyze weather, load, and other dynamic factors to accurately predict power generation and optimize operating parameters (temperature, current density, feed flow, *etc.*); (2) Developing intelligent control strategies to achieve synergistic operation of SOEC and renewable energy sources, including optimization of energy distribution, regulation of hydrogen production, and balancing of system efficiency; (3) Build power prediction and thermal field dynamic models to achieve power fluctuation smoothing and accurate temperature regulation. This multi-level, systematic AI-driven approach not only accelerates the development process of high-performance SOECs, but also provides a reliable guarantee for their stable operation under renewable energy conditions, which strongly promotes the large-scale application of green hydrogen energy. With the continuous innovation and deepened application of AI technology, SOEC technology will play an increasingly important role in the future energy transition.

Despite the promising potential of ML to advance SOECs, several significant issues must be addressed to fully realize its impact. One of the primary concerns is the inadequate standardization of data collection protocols[[37](#B37)]. Currently, there is a lack of unified standards for collecting, processing, and sharing data related to SOECs. These results are in inconsistent data quality and formats, making it difficult to integrate datasets from different sources and limiting the ability to train robust and generalizable ML models. The standardization of data collection protocols is essential to ensure that data is consistent, reliable, and interoperable across different research groups and platforms. Another critical issue is the lack of interpretability and low accuracy of AI models. Many existing AI models used in SOEC research are complex and often act as black boxes, making it challenging to understand how predictions are derived[[8](#B8),[11](#B11),[39](#B39)]. This lack of transparency hampers the trust and adoption of ML models by researchers and engineers. Moreover, the accuracy of these models often remains insufficient, with a lack of empirical support and specific examples to demonstrate their reliability and effectiveness. Detailed discussions and empirical studies are needed to validate the accuracy of ML models and provide specific examples of how they can be interpreted to guide practical applications. For instance, more research is required to show how AI models can be used to predict the long-term stability of SOEC or to optimize the composition of key materials with high precision.

Addressing these challenges requires a concerted effort to develop standardized data collection protocols and enhance the interpretability and accuracy of ML models. For instance, to clarify the degradation mechanism of SOEC, integrating *in-situ* analysis techniques, physically informed models, and high-quality databases is highly needed. *In-situ* analysis techniques, such as *in-situ* X-ray diffraction (XRD), electron microscopy, and spectroscopy, provide real-time data on the structural and chemical changes occurring in SOEC operation. This data can be used to train AI models, ensuring that they capture the dynamic behavior of oxides under actual operating conditions. These techniques also reveal the underlying mechanisms of degradation, such as phase transformation, microstructure evolution, and chemical degradation, which can be incorporated into ML models to improve their accuracy. Physically informed models integrate fundamental physical laws and principles related to SOEC degradation, such as the Nernst equation, Butler-Volmer equation, and heat transfer equations. This ensures that the models are grounded in the fundamental physics of the system. Multi-physics modeling approaches can account for the coupled effects of electrochemistry, thermodynamics, and mechanics in SOECs, providing a more comprehensive understanding of degradation mechanisms. High-quality databases are crucial for training reliable ML models. These databases should obey the standardizing data collection protocols, ensuring that the data is consistent and reliable, improving the quality of the training datasets. By overcoming these issues, AI can become a more powerful and reliable tool for advancing SOECs, ultimately contributing to the development of more efficient and sustainable green hydrogen production technologies.

## CONCLUSION

The integration of AI technologies with SOEC development represents a paradigm shift in accelerating green hydrogen production technologies. AI-driven approaches have demonstrated remarkable advances in key materials discovery and performance optimization, significantly reducing development cycles and costs. In the future, the continuous advancement of AI capabilities, coupled with expanding experimental datasets, will further enhance our ability to design high-performance, stable, and cost-effective SOEC single cells and systems. # How AI can accelerate the transition to Green hydrogen

July 12, 2023

3 min read | [Shafeen Nayan Veettil Muhammed](https://blog.se.com/author/shafeen-nayan-veettil-muhammed/)

In his 1874 book The Mysterious Island, Jules Verne described a world where “*water will one day be employed as fuel, that hydrogen and oxygen which constitute it, used singly or together, will furnish an inexhaustible source of heat and light, of an intensity of which coal is not capable*.”

149 years later, water is poised to play a crucial role in decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors. When [renewable energy](https://blog.se.com/energy-management-energy-efficiency/renewable-energy/) (RE) sources like wind, solar, hydropower splits water into two molecules — H2 and O2 — with no or very low carbon emissions— the result is termed **[Green hydrogen](https://blog.se.com/industry/2023/03/14/accelerate-green-hydrogen-implementation-with-digital-solutions/)**.

The primary purpose of Green hydrogen is to decarbonize sectors which are hard to electrify. We have technologies and solutions ready in the form of direct electrification and digitization in other applications/sectors (low temperature, light vehicles, etc.). Our focus and energy must be to develop innovative technologies to decarbonize the hard-to-abate processes.

## **Possible synergy between Green hydrogen and Artificial Intelligence for a sustainable future**

**Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a critical lever in transforming these challenges into solutions** across the different stages of the Green hydrogen project:

AI enabled simulation enables optimal sizing and operation strategy and provides visibility to end-to-end economic (LCOH), technical and greenhouse gas emission details at the conception stage, incorporating different vectors like renewable energy models, weather forecasts, electricity market price, electrolyzer models, balance of plants, and hydrogen derivatives.

With the integration of predictive AI analyzing vast amounts of data, we can optimize wind turbine operation in real-time, adjusting blade pitch and other electronic parameters to maximize energy production and reduce wear and tear.

An EMS advisor is a **cloud-based AI platform** that interface with :

**AI based Predictive Control algorithms of EMS** deploy the Green hydrogen plant’s optimization strategy considering:

## **Nobody Should Blindly Trust AI**

AI being applied to of Green hydrogen projects handle a lot of complex data to make smart and timely decisions. That said, AI algorithms are ‘black-box’ needing interpretability and explanation so that its decisions can be explained to various stakeholders. These AI algorithms are developed by human developers, trained using imperfect data, and are likely to be biased. Hence, there is a necessity to have ‘Explainable AI’.

![](https://blog.se.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/image-7.png)

[Explainable Artificial Intelligence](https://www.ibm.com/watson/explainable-ai) (XAI) will enable users to interrogate the system, receive feedback, understand why it suggested specific courses of action and modify their approach if necessary The integration of AI into the Green hydrogen value chain can **optimize the levelized cost of hydrogen (LCOH),** thereby significantly contributing to Green hydrogen becoming more competitive with traditional fossil fuels, creating progress toward a more sustainable energy future.

## **Learn More**

Schneider Electric and our strategic partners cover the entire Green hydrogen value chain with proven solutions to address challenges.

To learn more about how Schneider Electric can support your Green hydrogen development efforts, check out our Green hydrogen page under [Energies and Chemicals Solutions](https://www.se.com/ww/en/work/solutions/for-business/energies-and-chemicals/).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Artificial intelligence enhances hydrogen production efficiency by predicting demand, optimizing electrolysis processes, and improving catalyst design, leading to reduced costs and increased system reliability.

Integrating renewable energy sources with hydrogen production systems optimizes performance, leveraging AI to manage energy flows, enhance stability, and ensure seamless operation across fluctuating conditions.
The global green hydrogen market is projected to grow at over 31% CAGR by 2032, with significant investments aimed at scaling production and enhancing sustainability in energy sectors.
